entry.title: Retail, fashion and vertical business in SAP Cloud ERP 2602
entry.content:
The 2602 release of SAP Cloud ERP for Retail, Fashion & Vertical Business continues our focus on delivering innovations that simplify core retail processes, elevate operational efficiency, and strengthen omnichannel execution.

As retailers navigate an increasingly dynamic environment with more channels, more data, and higher customer expectations, this release brings capabilities designed to make daily operations smarter, faster, and more resilient.

Here is a quick summary of some the key highlights in a short video:

Let’s dive into these 4  key highlights that will help you deliver impact at scale.

Joule in Assortment Module Management

The first innovation introduces SAP Joule as a digital assistant within Assortment Module Management. With this enhancement, retail planners can complete assortment tasks simply by using natural language prompts.

Instead of navigating through multiple applications or relying on deep technical knowledge of assortment structures, users can ask Joule to create new assortments, assign products or locations, adjust validity periods, or terminate existing modules. Joule interprets these instructions and automatically triggers the appropriate system actions, making the overall process faster and more intuitive.

This conversational interface preserves the underlying operational logic while translating user intent into guided and executable steps. As a result, planners can manage assortment modules more efficiently, reduce their reliance on expert users, and spend more time on strategic merchandising decisions. The feature also lays a foundation for further AI‑driven enhancements in future releases.

 

Real-Time Inventory with OSTA Integration

Another important enhancement in the 2602 release is the ability to display real-time inventory by using unprocessed sales data from SAP Omnichannel Sales Transfer and Audit (OSTA). Traditionally, POS transactions are consolidated and posted to the ERP system in batches, meaning inventory figures shown during store operations could lag behind the actual stock situation.

With the new capability,  SAP Cloud ERP can now read unprocessed sales directly from OSTA, allowing the system to reflect accurate on-hand quantities throughout the business day.

This means that tasks such as looking up product availability, ordering products, transferring stock, or performing physical inventory counts all benefit from up-to-date inventory that accounts for ongoing store sales.

The enhancement improves inventory precision, reduces operational discrepancies, supports better decision-making during in-store processes, and helps deliver a consistent and reliable customer experience.

 

 

Selling from Store with Down Payment Processing

The third innovation strengthens the Manage In-Store Sales Orders app by introducing streamlined support for down payment scenarios. Retailers selling high-value goods often require customers to make partial or full payments before fulfillment begins.

With 2602, store associates can enter down payment information directly during sales order creation. The system supports both fixed-amount and percentage-based down payments and automatically structures the process using a delivery-based billing plan.

A single-click function generates the Down Payment Request invoice, enabling store teams to complete order entry and initiate payment in one smooth workflow. For retailers that consistently use 100% down payments, a dedicated application is available that simplifies the process even further.

This enhancement increases store productivity, reduces manual errors, and supports more effective financial control in scenarios requiring customer commitment before fulfillment.

 

Omnichannel Sales Promotions in Sales Orders via OPPS

The final highlight of the 2602 release is the foundational integration of SAP Omnichannel Promotion Pricing Service (OPPS) into sales order processing. With this integration, SAP Cloud ERP can trigger the OPPS service during sales order creation to determine whether a customer is eligible for specific promotions. When promotions apply, OPPS returns all relevant details, such as discounts or free goods, and the system updates the sales order automatically.

This integration makes it possible to support complex promotion types, including Bonus Buy scenarios, which go beyond the capabilities of native promotional pricing in Cloud ERP. Because OPPS is also used by POS and e-commerce channels, the integration ensures that customers experience consistent promotional pricing across all touchpoints.

While the initial foundation is delivered in 2602, the functional scope will continue to expand in future releases, enabling fully unified omnichannel promotion management.

 

### Stay Connected

These are just a few of the highlights from the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2602 release. We are dedicated to continuously enhancing our retail solution to meet the dynamic needs of your business.

To explore these features in more detail, please check out the resources below:

  • Official product page: SAP website | Partner portal (log in needed)
  • Full retail solution processes: Process Navigator – SAP for Me
  • Learning journey: Exploring SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, retail, fashion and vertical business
  • End-2-End Retail Demo: Interactive Value Journey

️

Watch the replays of our SAP Cloud ERP 2602 Early Release Series! 

From January 12 to January 16, 2026, we hosted a series of 22 expert-led live sessions to highlight the exciting innovations shipped with the 2602 release of SAP Cloud ERP. Don't miss this chance and watch them on demand–anywhere, anytime. 

Find all our assets 

We at Cloud ERP Product Success offer a service as versatile as our product itself. Check out the numerous offerings our team has created for you below:

entry.title: Two-Tier ERP with SAP Cloud ERP - 2602 Release Highlights
entry.content:
I am pleased to share the new addition from Two-Tier ERP as part of the 2602 release for  SAP Cloud ERP through this blog. I will be walking you through the scenarios from this release through the release video. We're delivering effective ways to enhance strategic flexibility, streamline complex processes, and build an agile enterprise for you.

Let's dive into the highlights,

The Strategic Foundation: Mastering Your Data in a Two-Tier ERP World 

Many organizations operate in a Two-Tier ERP landscape, with headquarters running on SAP Cloud ERP Private  (SAP S/4HANA Cloud, private edition). and agile subsidiaries operating on  SAP Cloud ERP, i.e., SAP S/4HANA Cloud, public edition.

In a product-centric two-tier ERP setup, master data objects such as customer and product master data become critical prerequisites for both tiers. This is where SAP Master Data Governance provides a strategic foundation.

 

By running SAP MDG at headquarters, organizations establish a single source of truth where all master data is created, validated, and approved before being distributed to target systems.

This approach follows a simple but crucial principle:

Create and govern master data centrally. Consume it locally.

Subsidiaries running on SAP Cloud ERP receive governed, high-quality master data, ensuring clean, compliant operations. This not only strengthens group-level reporting but also significantly accelerates the onboarding of new acquisitions. As your organization grows, your master data remains a stable and trusted asset.

For a deeper exploration of this topic, I recommend the blog post “SAP Master Data Governance in a Two-Tier ERPLandscape with HQ & Subsidiary Approach” by Abhijit Bolakhe.

 
Advanced Warehousing Meets Cloud Agility

Another flexible architectural approach we support is integrating a decentralized SAP Extended Warehouse Management (EWM) system with SAP Cloud ERP. This hybrid model allows you to maintain a clean-core cloud ERP while leveraging the rich, mature, and highly configurable functionalities of a full-scale EWM for your complex warehouse operations.

 Let's look at a typical process. For inbound goods, a purchase order in SAP Cloud ERP creates an inbound delivery that is sent to your decentralized EWM. EWM then orchestrates the entire physical process from unloading to put-away. Once complete, confirmations flow back to the cloud ERP, perfectly updating stock and financials.

Similarly, for outbound, a sales order triggers the process, with EWM managing everything from picking to loading, and finally sending the goods issue confirmation back to SAP S/4HANA Cloud. This integration offers a flexible yet perfectly balanced approach, combining advanced warehouse power with cloud simplicity.
The 2602 release for SAP Cloud ERP continues our commitment to providing a flexible, intelligent ERP. For wider types of industry, these updates deliver the strategic control, process efficiency, and commercial agility needed to thrive.
Please make it a point to deep dive into these EWM integration scenarios. We have two blog posts by Sahil Grover  The SAP Extended Warehouse Management Integration with SAP Cloud ERP - Part 1 and The SAP Extended Warehouse Management Integration with SAP Cloud ERP - Part 2

I hope this overview has been insightful.

Thank you for reading.

entry.title: Core Logistics in SAP Cloud ERP 2602
entry.content:
Welcome to the 2602 release of SAP Cloud ERP where we have introduced a set of enhancements focused on strengthening day-to-day supply chain execution and planning. These updates help streamline key logistics activities and improve user experience, so teams can execute faster, improve material visibility, and make more reliable, data-driven decisions across the end-to-end logistics flow.  In this blog post, you'll find an overview of the key new innovations and highlight features being rolled out with this release.

Short on time? The below video gives a summary of the top 3 highlights from this release.


 

 



Inventory Management

In the inventory management area, new features and apps have bene introduced to improve efficiency and accuracy when managing and moving physical inventory.

Streamlining Physical Inventory Management with new app "Manage Physical Inventory Item List" 

Managing physical inventory is about far more than counting stock: it’s about ensuring every item is tracked, verified, and processed correctly across potentially hundreds or thousands of line items, multiple locations, and multiple documents. In this kind of environment, speed and accuracy are critical. With this in mind, in the 2602 release of SAP Cloud ERP, SAP introduces the new Fiori app Manage Physical Inventory Item List (F8634)—giving inventory managers one clear, powerful place to manage physical inventory items across all relevant documents. The new app is designed to bring clarity and structure to this time-sensitive process by offering intuitive, user-friendly list reports and comprehensive status visibility so users can immediately see what needs attention and what’s already complete.

When you open the app, you start with flexible filtering options that let you tailor the list to the exact scenario you’re working on—such as narrowing down by plant and storage location to generate an overview for a specific area. Filters can be expanded easily, making it simple to adapt the screen to different use cases. From the resulting list, you can select items for further processing and execute key follow-up actions directly in the same place—including posting, triggering a recount, or printing—without jumping between applications. This is where the app really shines: it enables managers to filter physical inventory items by processing status (for example, showing only Counted items) so they can focus on the next required step and move work forward efficiently across multiple inventory documents.

A typical end-to-end scenario highlights the productivity gains. After physical inventory documents have been created and warehouse staff have completed counting, the inventory manager can filter by planned count date and then refine the list by status—such as showing only counted items. From there, it’s easy to review count details, identify quantity differences, and decide the right follow-up action. Items with no difference can be mass-selected and posted in just a click. Items with large discrepancies can be selected and sent for recount, where the user defines a recount date and (if needed) adds header information—automatically generating a new physical inventory document so warehouse teams know exactly what to recount. For marginal differences, the manager can still proceed with posting based on business rules, and if the system is configured with reason codes, the app prompts users to select the appropriate code during posting—supporting compliant, auditable processing aligned with company requirements. Finally, managers can confirm outcomes by filtering for posted items and, when needed, navigate directly from the list to the created material documents for review and further processing.

Overall, Manage Physical Inventory Item List (F8634) increases inventory transparency and helps teams complete inventory processing faster and with fewer errors. By combining comprehensive list reporting, efficient cross-document processing, and status-driven filtering in a single, streamlined experience, the app supports inventory managers with the clarity they need to keep physical inventory moving from count to resolution—accurately, quickly, and with full control.

 

 

Supporting Intra-Plant Stock Transfer Reservations

In many plants, materials have to be moved between storage locations to keep production and operations running—often at short notice and under time pressure. While these movements are typically planned using reservations (created manually or generated by MRP), execution has historically required warehouse staff to switch between apps or rely on classic processing such as MIGO—adding effort and increasing the risk of mistakes. With this new enhancement, intra-plant stock transfers become significantly easier and faster: warehouse teams can now use the Manage Reservation Items app to post stock transfers directly from a reservation for both manual and MRP-generated reservations using movement type 311, all in one place.

In practice, users filter and review the relevant reservation items, select multiple lines at once, and create the goods movement as a single, streamlined action—completing any missing details as needed before posting. Once posted, the system automatically creates two material document entries—one for the goods issue and one for the goods receipt—so the transfer is fully captured and traceable without extra steps. The result is clear business value: mass posting of several reservation items in one go, improved usability through integrated goods movement posting, and more time-efficient processing for busy warehouse teams.

 

Monitor Material Documents More Effectively : Material Documents Overview Enhancements 

SAP S/4HANA Cloud ERP’s Material Documents Overview app (F1077) is a modern SAP Fiori replacement for the classic MB51 experience—built to help you review and analyze goods movements with a smoother, more intuitive UX while closing remaining functional gaps step by step. In one place, users can search, filter, sort, and drill into material documents to understand what moved, when it posted, and how it impacts operations and stock. This transition is more than “nice to have”: moving to the new app is essential because legacy options will ultimately be deprecated, and SAP is actively enhancing F1077 based on real customer feedback to make switching worthwhile.

Driven directly by customer input, SAP has been evolving the app’s functional scope and usability to better match how supply chain and warehouse teams actually work. Recent enhancements include the availability of additional fields—notably amount in local currency and sales value—to make financial and commercial context visible alongside logistics data. The app also now supports signed quantity, reducing the mental gymnastics of interpreting directionality across receipts and issues. And for users who regularly pivot between quantity-focused and value-focused analysis, SAP introduced a variant that displays amounts and values, making it easier to switch perspectives and tailor the overview to different roles and reporting needs.

In the 2602 release, the app takes a more visual and analytical leap forward with two planned improvements that make high-volume review work noticeably smoother. First, color coding for selected quantities (such as Signed Quantity)helps users identify the right figures at a glance, reducing errors and speeding up interpretation during daily checks. Second, sums for quantities and values add instant rollups for quick validation and comparison—particularly useful when reconciling multiple postings or checking totals across filtered selections. Together, these 2602 features continue the push to improve usability while enhancing the functional scope in the goods movement process, keeping the Material Documents Overview app firmly positioned as a practical tool for operational control in SAP Cloud ERP.

For further details, check out this blog by @stefanie_bierbach 

 

 

BETA Testing : AI-Assisted Goods Receipt Processing


For interested customers, we are offering early access to test and evaluate our new AI-Assisted goods receipt posting functionality before it is made generally available for productive use.  Participating in testing is completely free of charge and requires only that you sign a test and evaluation agreement following registration. If you are interested in participating, sign up here.

 The Fiori app “Post Goods Receipt for Purchasing Document” streamlines the move from paper to digital by letting users simply photograph a delivery note instead of manually entering its data. After the photo is taken, the document is processed automatically using OCR and a Large Language Model to extract the relevant information. If the extracted data matches an open purchase order, the app prepopulates the goods receipt with highlighted fields and open items, so the user only needs to confirm the receipt quantity before posting the material document. The result is a faster, more flexible, and scalable goods receipt process that also shows how GenAI can be quickly extended to many other Fiori applications.

 

 


Handling Unit Management

Effective Handling Unit Management is fundamental to an integrated and transparent supply chain. Our latest updates to SAP Cloud ERP introduce significant enhancements in this area, designed to increase operational flexibility, improve system integration, and ensure alignment with global standards

Terminology Alignment with Global Standards for SSCC

To promote clarity and ensure consistency with global industry practices, we have updated the terminology used in the configuration activities for Serial Shipping Container Code (SSCC) numbering. The term "ILN basis number" has been replaced with "GS1 company prefix." This alignment with current GS1 standards helps reduce ambiguity during system setup and ensures your organizations handling unit identification is compliant with international norms. This change enhances usability for global enterprises without altering any underlying system logic.

Interface Support for Empty Handling Unit Creation

To provide greater flexibility for integration scenarios, the OData API for Handling Unit Management now supports the creation of empty handling units. This enhancement enables external systems or custom applications to first create a handling unit container and then subsequently manage the packing of materials into it. This capability allows organizations to model real-world logistics processes more accurately, where containers are often prepared before their contents are finalized. This facilitates more sophisticated and responsive integration with warehouse automation systems and third-party logistics platforms.

 

New Business Event for Handling Unit Creation

We are introducing a new business event that is triggered upon the creation of a handling unit within an Inventory Management-managed storage location. This event-driven mechanism enables real-time communication with external systems, such as parcel service providers or transportation management platforms, instantly notifying them when a handling unit is ready. This capability allows organizations to automate downstream processes, improve the timeliness of data exchange, and enhance end-to-end visibility, thereby reducing manual coordination and improving overall supply chain responsiveness.

 

Integration with External Output Management Systems

This release introduces a new feature that allows for the integration of Handling Unit Management with external, best-of-breed output management systems. By enabling the external channel in your output parameter determination, you can now send raw data for handling unit labels and documents directly to a preferred third-party solution. This provides organizations with maximum flexibility to design complex form layouts, manage specialized printing hardware, and utilize advanced output channels. The external system processes the output and sends a status confirmation back to SAP S/4HANA Cloud, ensuring full process traceability and control.

 

 

Further Services to Support Partial Goods Receipt for Inbound Delivery with Third-Party Logistics Warehouse Management (Release 2508.2) 

We are excited to introduce a significant enhancement to our warehouse logistics capabilities: the ability to post partial goods receipts for inbound deliveries from your third-party Warehouse Management System (WMS). This feature directly addresses a common bottleneck at the receiving dock by empowering your warehouse staff to begin the putaway process immediately after unloading parts of a delivery, rather than waiting to check the entire shipment. This new flexibility accommodates a wide range of scenarios, allowing you to process complete or fractional quantities for single or multiple delivery items, both with and without handling units. This ultimately accelerates inventory availability and dramatically improves warehouse throughput.

To ensure seamless data integrity, this process is supported by robust, automated communication between SAP S/4HANA Cloud and your WMS. For every partial receipt posted, a confirmation is sent to the WMS, and a straightforward process allows for the cancellation of any erroneous postings. Once all parts of the delivery have been received, the inbound delivery in SAP S/4HANA Cloud is automatically updated to reflect the final, accurate totals for quantity, weight, and volume. A final confirmation is then sent to the WMS, guaranteeing that your core ERP system is perfectly synchronized with the physical reality documented by your warehouse partner.

📽️ Watch the replays of our SAP Cloud ERP 2602 Early Release Series!

From January 12-16, we hosted a series of 22 expert-led live sessions to highlight the exciting innovations shipped with the SAP Cloud ERP 2602 release. Missed the live sessions? We've got you covered! Take advantage of the entire series or pick and choose the sessions most relevant to you. You will find the recording and presentation for Enterprise Portfolio and Project Management as well as 21 other topics of interest. Don't miss this chance and watch them on demand – anywhere, anytime.

 

🔎Find all our assets

We at Cloud ERP and SCM Product Success offer a service as versatile as our product itself. Check out the numerous offerings our team has created for you below:

entry.title: What's New in Supply Chain in SAP Cloud ERP 2602?
entry.content:
In this blog, we're excited to introduce you to some of the most impactful updates for Supply Chain in the Cloud ERP 2602 release. This update is all about empowering you with greater visibility, streamlined processes, and enhanced flexibility across your entire supply chain. Whether you work in inventory, warehousing, or transportation, these new features are designed to make your daily work more efficient and intelligent. Below, you'll find a summary of the main highlights from each area of our supply chain solution, and if you follow the links, you'll find a comprehensive overview of further new features in each area from our expert product managers.


2602 Release Highlights Overview

To kick things off, we've put together an overview of our top  supply chain highlights in the 2602 release of SAP Cloud ERP.

 

Warehouse Management in SAP Cloud ERP 2602

One fresh Warehouse Management feature we're keen to highlight is the new Joule capability to assist with your Warehouse Management Operations, which we'll look at below. Of course, there's much more to come -  make sure to check out @Christian_Ries blog for the full scope of release highlights in Warehouse Management in the 2602 release. 

Joule Enablement For Outbound Delivery Orders & Warehouse Tasks

Building on recent enhancements, we continue to expand the capabilities of Joule, our generative AI assistant. Now, Joule can assist users in accessing and managing key business objects in Warehouse Management using natural language. You can use simple conversational language to process delivery orders and warehouse tasks, ask questions, display data, and receive navigation links to relevant applications. Joule can also perform actions such as creating and updating business objects. This functionality simplifies user interaction and empowers your team to control warehouse operations with greater efficiency and accuracy, directly through a natural language interface.

 

Automatic Packing for Direct Goods Receipt Posting after Production Order Confirmation 

A recent key enhancement automates the packing of goods following production. Previously, a direct goods receipt could be posted automatically upon confirmation of a production order. Now, this process has been enhanced to include automatic packing. The system can be configured to automatically pack any unpacked products into handling units based on predefined packaging instructions. This action is triggered during the confirmation of a manufacturing order, eliminating the need for manual user input. This feature increases efficiency on the production floor by combining automatable steps and improves accuracy by providing total automation from confirmation to packed stock, which reduces the potential for human error.

 

Transportation Management in SAP Cloud ERP 2602

In the below demo, @Markus_Oertelt talks you through the complete transportation lifecycle associated with subcontracting purchase orders. This seamless integration is released in 2602 and enables transportation planning, execution and freight settlement via subcontractors with minimal manual effort and reduced scope for errors. Of course, that's not all, and for an overview of further TM highlights in this release, check this blog out from TM Expert @thomas_roesch.

 

Enable Subcontracting Purchase Order Integration With Transportation Management

This powerful new feature enables seamless integration of subcontracting purchase orders with Transportation Management (TM). Users can now manage the complete transportation lifecycle associated with the subcontracting process, from planning transportation demand based on purchase order requirements to executing movements and settling freight costs for both outbound and inbound processes. For outbound processes, transportation demand from the delivery to the subcontractor can be planned, executed, and settled. For inbound processes, the same applies to the delivery of the finished product. This integration streamlines the end-to-end process, reduces manual effort, ensures accurate cost allocation, and provides real-time visibility, allowing for greater control and transparency over subcontracting logistics.

 


Core Logistics in SAP Cloud ERP 2602

Under core logistics, we include the backbone of any well-oiled supply chain solution, featuring inventory management, delivery management, handling-unit & serial number management. With release 2602, we're particularly excited by the improved user experience when managing physical inventory delivered by the new app Manage Physical Inventory Items List. Of course, that's not all, and for some more core logistics highlights, look no further than the overview blog by @Greg_Hutcheon1. 

Introducing Manage Physical Inventory Item List (App F8634)


In the 2602 release of SAP Cloud ERP, SAP introduces the new Fiori app Manage Physical Inventory Item List (F8634) to help inventory managers process physical inventory faster and with greater accuracy. Instead of switching between multiple documents and apps, users get a single, structured list report with clear status visibility across all relevant physical inventory items, making it easy to see what’s pending, in progress, or complete.

The app offers flexible filters (for example by plant, storage location, planned count date, and processing status) so managers can quickly focus on the right subset of items—such as “Counted” entries ready for follow-up. From the same screen, they can execute key actions like posting, triggering recounts, or printing, including mass processing across multiple inventory documents. In a typical flow, managers review counted items, identify differences, post items with no variance in one step, and initiate recounts for major discrepancies by setting a recount date and generating a new physical inventory document. If configured, the app also supports compliant processing by prompting for reason codes during posting. Overall, F8634 improves transparency, reduces errors, and streamlines inventory processing from count to resolution.

 


Order Promising

New BAdI for Confirmation Acceptance Rule

In this release, SAP Cloud ERP added a powerful new extension point for Order Promising: the Confirmation Adjustment (Confirmation Acceptance) BAdI, also known as “adjust ATP confirmation.” It lets you take the standard confirmations calculated by SAP ATP—quantities and dates—then apply your own business logic on top after the basic ATP check.

With this BAdI, you can override confirmed quantities or shift confirmation dates to better match business priorities (like customer tiers, allocation rules, or operational constraints). The adjusted confirmation can be written back and persisted in the relevant tables, so downstream processes work with the business-approved result—not just the original ATP output.

To keep things safe and explainable, SAP includes validations to prevent over-confirmation, and the system records the custom outcome in the ATP check log for full transparency. The adjusted values can also influence BOP decisions(for example via FIXMG), giving you more control over how confirmations behave during backorder processing.

Supply Protection Indicator in the Display Product Availability App

The Display Product Availability app now includes an easy-to-understand Supply Protection (SUP) indicator with two states (on/off). It adds immediate context to the available quantity by showing whether that quantity may be subject to supply protection.

Note: The icon is shown as active when supply protection is enabled for the specific product in the specific plant.

A SUP icon now appears directly next to a product’s available quantity. It indicates whether Supply Protection is considered in the availability calculation, helping users quickly interpret what they’re seeing without extra checks. This improves day-to-day decision-making by giving users instant visibility into whether the displayed quantity is “straight availability” or potentially restricted by supply protection—saving time and reducing confusion during availability checks.

 

Summary

This blog provides an overview of the key features and functions delivered in the 2602 release of SAP Cloud ERP. Make sure to follow the links above to find about more supply chain features in depth with the relevant specialist blogs. Beyond supply chain capabilities, the 2602 SAP Cloud ERP release was packed full of great new features designed to drive success. For an overview of the key highlights from this release, make sure to check out the 2602 release blog by @BeSchulze  

We at Cloud ERP and SCM Product Success offer a service as versatile as our product itself. Click on the enablement wheel below and check out the numerous offerings our team has created for you:

entry.title: End-to-End Automatic Order to Billing Process in SAP SD (Low-Coding Approach)
entry.content:
In line with the customer requirement, an automatic process was designed covering delivery creation, goods issue posting, and billing immediately after sales order creation.

The solution was implemented based entirely on standard SAP SD principles and designed under a low-coding approach, minimizing custom developments while preserving full control over the process.

1. Excluding Specific Customers from the Automatic Process

During the analysis phase, it became necessary to exclude certain customers from the automated flow.

To achieve this:

  • A custom indicator field was defined under Business Partner – SD (FLCU01) view.
  • Using transaction CMOD, field KDGRP4 was renamed as “Automatic Billing Exclusion”.
  • For customers that must remain outside the automated process, value “X” is maintained via customizing.

This design ensures flexibility and allows business users to control participation in the automated process without additional development.

2. Sales Document Configuration (VOV8)

Within VOV8 sales document type configuration, the relevant document types were adjusted by activating the Immediate Delivery setting.

This configuration enables the system to automatically trigger delivery creation directly from the sales order.

3. Dynamic Control in MV45AFZZ (SAVE_DOCUMENT_PREPARE)

At enhancement point MV45AFZZ – SAVE_DOCUMENT_PREPARE, a class-based method was implemented instead of writing logic directly into the include.

The method dynamically reads Business Partner data and manipulates field TVAK-LISOF depending on defined conditions.

Key Logic Summary:

  • If the document creator is not the workflow user (SAP_WFRT), automation is cancelled.
  • KNVV-KVGR4 is checked for the exclusion flag.
  • If the customer is marked with “X”, the automation flag (TVAK-LISOF) is cleared dynamically.
  • Otherwise, the process continues automatically.

This approach ensures that:

  • Standard configuration remains intact.
  • Automation can be centrally controlled.
  • Sales document level settings can be programmatically overridden when necessary.

  

METHOD zsd_save_document_prepare_1.

* inserted by serdar ulgen. 19.01.2026 11:01:55 .

  i_knvv-kunnr = i_vbak-kunnr.
  i_knvv-vkorg = i_vbak-vkorg.
  i_knvv-vtweg = i_vbak-vtweg.
  i_knvv-spart = i_vbak-spart.

  FIELD-SYMBOLS <lv_lisof> TYPE any.
  ASSIGN ('(SAPMV45A)TVAK-LISOF') TO <lv_lisof>.
  IF sy-subrc <> 0.
    RETURN.
  ENDIF.

  IF i_vbak-ernam <> 'SAP_WFRT'.
    CLEAR <lv_lisof>.
    RETURN.
  ENDIF.

  SELECT SINGLE kvgr4
    FROM knvv
    INTO @i_knvv-kvgr4
    WHERE kunnr = @i_knvv-kunnr
      AND vkorg = @i_knvv-vkorg
      AND vtweg = @i_knvv-vtweg
      AND spart = @i_knvv-spart.

  IF i_knvv-kvgr4 = 'X' AND <lv_lisof> = 'A'.
    CLEAR <lv_lisof>.
  ENDIF.

* if u any question please contact with me.

 

4. Automating Goods Issue and Billing

After completing the Sales → Delivery flow, the next phase covered:

  • Goods Issue posting
  • Billing creation

Alternative Approach (Not Preferred)

An alternative solution could involve sequential BAPI calls for goods issue and billing within a custom program.

Although technically feasible, this method requires careful handling of:

  • ENQUEUE / DEQUEUE locks
  • Document synchronization
  • LUW management

Improper lock handling may lead to document conflicts.

 5. Leveraging Standard Output Type REA0

In this implementation, we leveraged the fact that standard SAP output type REA0 can directly trigger billing.

The approach:

  1. The standard program used in REA0 was copied.
  2. A Goods Issue BAPI call was added at the beginning.
  3. Billing creation was kept as standard.
  4. Output determination was assigned correctly to the relevant delivery types using VV21.

This step is critical — without correct output assignment, the automation will not execute.

6. Technical Highlights

  • Goods issue is triggered via WS_DELIVERY_UPDATE_2
  • Billing is created via /SPE/CREATE_NEW_BILLING_CALL
  • Error handling is managed through NAST protocol updates
  • Separate LUW handling is ensured via /SPE/CALL_PROC_IN_NEW_LUW
  • Minimal custom coding
  • Maximum reuse of SAP standard structures
REPORT  zsd_***_****.

TABLES: nast.
CONSTANTS: c_allready_billed TYPE fkstk VALUE 'C'.
DATA: gs_nast            TYPE nast,
      lt_delivery_data   TYPE leshp_delivery_t,
      ls_delivery_data   TYPE LINE OF leshp_delivery_t,
      lt_delivery_items  TYPE leshp_delivery_item_t,
      ls_doc_pos_ver_req TYPE /spe/doc_pos_ver_req,
      lt_doc_pos_ver_req TYPE /spe/doc_pos_ver_req_t,
      lv_dummy_message   TYPE bapiret2-message,
      lt_bapiret2        TYPE bapiret2_t,
      ls_bapiret2        TYPE bapiret2,
      ls_leshp_data      TYPE leshp_data.

FIELD-SYMBOLS: <ls_billing_req> TYPE LINE OF leshp_delivery_item_t.

*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*&      Form  ENTRY
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*       create new bill
*----------------------------------------------------------------------*
FORM entry USING returncode  TYPE sy-subrc
                 use_screen  TYPE c.

  DATA: ls_vbeln_vl TYPE vbeln_vl,
        lv_leave    TYPE c,                                 "n_1238590
        lv_error    TYPE c,
        ls_vbkok    TYPE vbkok,
        lt_prot     TYPE TABLE OF prott,
        lv_text     TYPE t100-text,
        lv_delivery TYPE vbeln_vl.

  FIELD-SYMBOLS: <ls_return> TYPE bapiret2.

  IF sy-uname = 'SAP_WFRT'.

  lv_delivery = nast-objky.
  ls_vbkok-vbeln_vl  = lv_delivery.
  ls_vbkok-wadat_ist = sy-datum.
  ls_vbkok-wabuc     = abap_true.

  CALL FUNCTION 'WS_DELIVERY_UPDATE_2'
    EXPORTING
      synchron      = abap_true
      vbkok_wa      = ls_vbkok
      delivery      = lv_delivery
    TABLES
      prot          = lt_prot
    EXCEPTIONS
      error_message = 1
      OTHERS        = 2.

  LOOP AT lt_prot ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<error>).
    IF <error>-msgty = 'E' OR <error>-msgty = 'A'.

      SELECT SINGLE text FROM t100 INTO @LV_text WHERE sprsl = @SY-langu AND arbgb = @<error>-msgid AND msgnr = @<error>-msgno.

      IF sy-subrc = 0 AND lv_text IS NOT INITIAL.
        MESSAGE lv_text TYPE 'E'.
      ELSE.
        MESSAGE ID <error>-msgid TYPE 'E' NUMBER <error>-msgno.
      ENDIF.

      returncode = 1.
      RETURN.
    ENDIF.
  ENDLOOP.

ENDIF.


* Redirect this NAST processing to separate LUW - in case it is
* called with dispatching time 4 (immediate processing)
* during update task                                        "v_n_1238590
CALL FUNCTION '/SPE/CALL_PROC_IN_NEW_LUW'
  EXPORTING
    is_nast  = nast
  IMPORTING
    ef_leave = lv_leave.
IF NOT lv_leave IS INITIAL.
*   Per default we set the status on error, so that it can be reprocessed
*   in error processing (RSNAST0F) if there is any syntax error / update
*   termination / ... in the decoupled NAST processing.
  returncode = 4.
  RETURN.
ENDIF.                                                    "^_n_1238590

returncode = 0.
* Get Item Data of Delivery
*  ls_leshp_data-item = 'X'.                                  "n_1822545
* Get Status of Outbound Delivery
ls_leshp_data-head_status = 'X'.
* Copy Nast Entry
gs_nast = nast.
ls_vbeln_vl = nast-objky.

CALL FUNCTION 'LE_DELIVERY_GET_BUFFERED'
  EXPORTING
    if_vbeln               = ls_vbeln_vl
    is_data                = ls_leshp_data
  CHANGING
    cx_deliveries          = lt_delivery_data
  EXCEPTIONS
    no_item_selected       = 1
    selected_item_enqueued = 2
    no_key_specified       = 3
    OTHERS                 = 4.
IF sy-subrc <> 0.
  MESSAGE e267(/spe/vl) WITH ls_vbeln_vl INTO lv_dummy_message.
  returncode = sy-subrc.
ELSE.

* only one delivery can be found by premisses (VBELN).
  READ TABLE lt_delivery_data INDEX 1 INTO ls_delivery_data.
  IF sy-subrc = 0.
    IF ls_delivery_data-head_status-fkstk = c_allready_billed.
* Allready billed.
      returncode = 0.
      MESSAGE i265(/spe/vl) WITH ls_vbeln_vl INTO lv_dummy_message.
    ELSE.
*        lt_delivery_items = ls_delivery_data-item.         "v_n_1822545
*        LOOP AT lt_delivery_items ASSIGNING <ls_billing_req>.
*          MOVE-CORRESPONDING <ls_billing_req> TO ls_doc_pos_ver_req.
*          APPEND ls_doc_pos_ver_req TO lt_doc_pos_ver_req.
*        ENDLOOP.
      ls_doc_pos_ver_req-vbeln = ls_delivery_data-head-vbeln.
      APPEND ls_doc_pos_ver_req TO lt_doc_pos_ver_req.    "^_n_1822545

      CALL FUNCTION '/SPE/CREATE_NEW_BILLING_CALL'
        EXPORTING
          it_billing_req     = lt_doc_pos_ver_req
          iv_version_checked = 'X'
        IMPORTING
          et_return          = lt_bapiret2.
      LOOP AT lt_bapiret2 ASSIGNING <ls_return>.
        IF <ls_return>-type = 'E'.
          lv_error = 'X'.
        ENDIF.
      ENDLOOP.
      IF lv_error = 'X'.
        returncode = 1.
        use_screen = ' '.
      ELSE.
        returncode = 0.
      ENDIF.
    ENDIF.

  ELSE.
* No Entry found !!!
    MESSAGE e267(/spe/vl) WITH ls_vbeln_vl INTO lv_dummy_message.
    returncode = 9.
  ENDIF.
ENDIF.
CALL FUNCTION 'NAST_PROTOCOL_INITIALIZE'.
LOOP AT lt_bapiret2 ASSIGNING <ls_return>.
  IF <ls_return>-type = 'E'.
    lv_error = 'X'.
  ENDIF.
  PERFORM protocol_update_return USING <ls_return>.
ENDLOOP.
IF lv_error = 'X'.
  returncode = 1.
  use_screen = ' '.
ELSE.
  returncode = 0.
ENDIF.

IF sy-subrc <> 0.
  PERFORM protocol_update.
ENDIF.
ENDFORM.                    " ENTRY

*---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*       FORM PROTOCOL_UPDATE                                          *
*---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*       The messages are collected for the processing protocol.       *
*---------------------------------------------------------------------*

FORM protocol_update.

  CALL FUNCTION 'NAST_PROTOCOL_UPDATE'
    EXPORTING
      msg_arbgb = syst-msgid
      msg_nr    = syst-msgno
      msg_ty    = syst-msgty
      msg_v1    = syst-msgv1
      msg_v2    = syst-msgv2
      msg_v3    = syst-msgv3
      msg_v4    = syst-msgv4
    EXCEPTIONS
      OTHERS    = 1.

ENDFORM.                    "PROTOCOL_UPDATE
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*&      Form  protocol_update_return
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*       text
*----------------------------------------------------------------------*
*      -->P_<LS_RETURN>  text
*----------------------------------------------------------------------*
FORM protocol_update_return USING p_return TYPE bapiret2.

  CALL FUNCTION 'NAST_PROTOCOL_UPDATE'
    EXPORTING
      msg_arbgb = p_return-id
      msg_nr    = p_return-number
      msg_ty    = p_return-type
      msg_v1    = p_return-message_v1
      msg_v2    = p_return-message_v2
      msg_v3    = p_return-message_v3
      msg_v4    = p_return-message_v4
    EXCEPTIONS
      OTHERS    = 1.

ENDFORM.                    " protocol_update_return

7. Final Architecture Principle

Standard-first approach

Low-coding strategy

Dynamic control via enhancement

Output-driven automation

Controlled customer-based exclusion

Lock-safe execution

8. Conclusion

This solution delivers a fully automated Order → Delivery → Goods Issue → Billing process while maintaining:

  • High adherence to SAP standards
  • Minimal custom development footprint
  • Flexible customer-level control
  • System stability and scalability

By combining standard configuration (VOV8, VV21), enhancement logic (MV45AFZZ), and output-based processing (REA0), an end-to-end automated and maintainable SD process was successfully implemented.

entry.title: Why SELECT returns different rows when querying a CDS View on MSEG versus MSEG directly
entry.content:
• Introduction
  • Custom CDS view ZCDS_E_MSEG
  • ABAP SELECT on MSEG
  • ABAP SELECT on ZCDS_E_MSEG
  • Why the Row Count Differs
  • Key Takeaways

 

Introduction

If you’ve recently worked with S/4HANA and created a custom CDS view on a standard table such as MSEG (*), you may have noticed something surprising:

Even if the CDS view is a simple SELECT FROM MSEG without any WHERE clause and there's no CDS Access Control implied, the number of rows returned by ABAP SELECT * FROM ZCDS_E_VIEW may not match the results of ABAP SELECT * FROM MSEG.

(*) You may find other tables like MSEG in the note 2206980 - Material Inventory Managment: change of data model in S/4HANA - SAP for Me.

 

Custom CDS view ZCDS_E_MSEG

@AbapCatalog.sqlViewName: 'ZCDS_V_MSEG'
define view ZCDS_E_MSEG as select from MSEG
{
  *
}

NB: a DDIC-based CDS view is used here to make the example simple, but as it's obsolete since ABAP 7.57 CDS view entities should be used instead.

 

ABAP SELECT on MSEG

SELECT * FROM MSEG
WHERE mblnr = '5000000000'
INTO TABLE (lt_mseg).
This example assumes there are some lines in MSEG for MBLNR = '5000000000'.
This ABAP SELECT may return more rows than the below ABAP SELECT on ZCDS_E_MSEG.
 

ABAP SELECT on ZCDS_E_MSEG

SELECT * FROM zcds_e_mseg
WHERE mblnr = '5000000000'
INTO TABLE (lt_cds_mseg).
Even though this query selects all rows from the CDS view ZCDS_E_MSEG (which is based on the physical MSEG table without any WHERE), the number of rows may be fewer than the ABAP SELECT on MSEG.
 

Why the Row Count Differs

  1. The table MSEG exists physically in the database but is no longer maintained

    • In S/4HANA, the real material document data lives in MATDOC.

    • The legacy table MSEG still exists in the database with the original ECC data, but is no more updated by S/4HANA transactions.

  2. ABAP SELECT on MSEG is redirected to the replacement object

    • When ABAP code does SELECT * FROM MSEG, SAP automatically redirects the query to the CDS replacement view NSDM_E_MSEG, which reads MATDOC.



      NB: NSDM_E_MSEG filters MATDOC using WHERE record_type = 'MDOC' but it's not implied in the problem discussed here.
      You can see in the SQL Trace (ST05) how ABAP SELECT ... FROM MSEG ... is automatically using the replacement object:

    • This redirection ensures that old programs continue to work correctly and return all material document lines, i.e. those lines migrated and updated in S/4HANA live.

  3. Custom CDS views on MSEG read the physical table directly

    • Any CDS view defined on MSEG does not use the replacement object, so it reads the actual content of the MSEG table, which are the data migrated from ECC, but are no more updated in S/4HANA.

    • If the table MSEG is empty in your system, it's because the Obsolete Data Handling tool has run (since S/4HANA 2023, it's the transaction code SODH, which has superseded ODH_DATA_PROCESSING). Reference: SAP S/4HANA Upgrade Guide, section Cleaning Up Obsolete Data After the Upgrade.

 

✅Key Takeaways

  • Do not rely on MSEG for new development; always use MATDOC or NSDM replacement views such as NSDM_E_MSEG.

  • NEVER use the old tables in your custom CDS views because they contain obsolete data.

  • After you have successfully tested and validated the upgrade results, you can clean up the obsolete data with the Obsolete Data Handling tool, to free space and to avoid the confusion and panic among developers.

entry.title: Introducing SAP Cloud ERP Private, Nonproduction Usage: A Flexible Path to SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud
entry.content:
Introduction

Many SAP ERP Central Component (SAP ECC) cloud customers face a necessary upgrade because of upcoming end-of-support deadlines for SAP ERP 6.0 private cloud systems on Enhancement Packages 6-8 (mainstream maintenance ending in 2027 and extended maintenance in 2030).

SAP ECC (SAP ECC 6.0 with Enhancement Package 6 to 8 ) will move to Customer Specific Maintenance starting 1 January 2031. This also means that the SAP ERP, private edition offering will effectively have an end of availability on 31 December 2030. Because of this, many customers will face a time-sensitive transition period. During this time, they may need to run both their existing SAP ERP 6.0 system and the new SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud system in parallel—especially for non-production environments like testing, development, and QA during migration.

Having both environments available can accelerate transformation initiatives, allowing teams to leverage bundled cloud components for innovation, testing, process redesign, and early adoption of new capabilities while the legacy system continues to support ongoing operations.

At the same time, customers seeking a more cost-optimized transition path now have a new option that provides earlier access to a non-production environment without requiring a full parallel subscription. This gives customers greater flexibility to plan and execute their transformation while managing overall subscription costs.

To support this approach, we are introducing SAP Cloud ERP Private, nonproduction usage. This offering enables unlimited non-production use of SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud during the transition period, allowing customers to perform migration and testing activities without incurring the cost of a full production subscription.

How this helps, for example.

Explanation:

  • Without SAP Cloud ERP Private, nonproduction usage 
    In Year 2, the SAP ERP, private edition subscription is needed for the production systems, but as non-productive environments are gradually upgraded to the new S/4HANA Private Cloud version, they also need SAP Cloud ERP Private subscription. This overlap leads to simultaneous subscription costs of both SAP ERP, private edition and new SAP Cloud ERP Private systems. 
  • With SAP Cloud ERP Private, nonproduction usage 
    In Year 2, production continues under the old SAP ERP, private edition subscription, while the non-productive environments are covered by the new SAP Cloud ERP Private, nonproduction usage product. SAP Cloud ERP Private, nonproduction usage can be applied to an unlimited number of non-productive systems, allowing full coverage without triggering a full double subscription. In Year 3, all environments have migrated and can now be covered with the normal SAP Cloud ERP Private subscription.

Benefits of SAP Cloud ERP Private, nonproduction usage  

  • Removes simultaneous subscriptions during the transition phase 
  • Supports testing and validation without the need for exact timing of the switch to a new subscription 
  • Access to the SAP AI portfolio in the nonproduction environment, not available with SAP ECC [SAP ERP 6.0]
  • You only pay for limited support for the non-productive systems during the migration period
  • Covers an unlimited number of non-productive systems 
  • Gives customers the flexibility to plan and execute the migration properly
  • The transition period can be as long as needed, keeping the end of support date in mind.
  • SAP Cloud ERP Private, nonproduction usage is an addition to the SAP ERP, private edition subscription, extending the product support to S/4HANA Private Cloud in the transition phase. 
  • Helps organizations meet upgrade deadlines without budget overruns

entry.title: Pregúntame lo que quieras con SAP Champions – Serie mensual (Sesión 2)
entry.content:
Hola querida SAP Community,

Mi nombre es María Antonieta López, SAP Champion de México.

Actualmente, trabajo como Directora de Programas SAP, enfocada en programas globales de transformación SAP, implementaciones de SAP S/4HANA, arquitectura empresarial, marcos de gobernanza e innovación digital dentro del ecosistema SAP.

Como parte de los SAP Champions que lideran la iniciativa “Ask Me Anything” junto con Sebastiano Marchesini,

Me complace informarles que yo estaré liderando la edición de marzo.🚀

Así que sí…

Pueden preguntarme. LO QUE SEA.

 

Después de más de 20 años viviendo dentro de transformaciones SAP, probablemente he visto lo bueno, lo malo… y esos momentos de “¿cómo sobrevivió este sistema al go-live?” 😅

 

Durante las próximas semanas estaré respondiendo cualquier pregunta que tengan — sin filtros — solo SAP en vivo y real.

💡Podemos explorar temas como:

⚙️Implementaciones de SAP S/4HANA y programas globales de transformación – Ejecución de implementaciones end-to-end de SAP S/4HANA y programas de transformación a gran escala, permitiendo a las organizaciones modernizar sus procesos de negocio centrales, estandarizar operaciones globales y adoptar capacidades digitales de nueva generación.

🏗️ Arquitectura Empresarial – Procesos, artefactos y estrategias para la arquitectura empresarial de SAP basada en TOGAF.

🧼 Clean Core en SAP Cloud ERP (Private & Public) — procesos de gestión de proyectos para implementar o crear extensiones o interfaces en tu proyecto.

(Hablando de BUILD para configuradores y su uso con Joule, APIs preconstruidas, APIs nuevas, y por supuesto nuestra plataforma como servicio SAP Integration Suite, todo dentro de nuestra querida SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP)).

☁️ RISE o GROW con SAP + Gobernanza y metodología Clean Core

Desde SAP Activate hasta marcos como SAP Application Extension Methodology, ISA-M (Integration Solution Advisory Methodology), SAP Design Thinking, SAP Data Architecture Framework y, por qué no… incluso SAP Business AI Framework.

Puedes preguntar sobre qué artefactos usar, cómo ejecutar los procesos o por qué tengo que hacer esta tarea... Y te diré lo que pasará si te los saltas... Muahaha! …💀 así que mis historias de terror, como el invierno… están por llegar.

🏛️ Cómocrear un SAP Center of Excellence (CoE) o brindar servicios de SAP AM (Application Management) y realmente vivir felices para siempre con SAP usando Cloud ALM + Focused Build + Activate.

🌍 Globalización en SAP, ya sea de forma general o por país. ¡Larga vida a SAP Document & Reporting Compliance!

Y también a las extensiones DRC de nuestros partners SAP.

🧭 Roadmaps de soluciones y estrategia de transformación, etc

¿Y cualquier otra cosa? y ... ¡si no sé la respuesta! 👉 Prometo encontrar a alguien que sí la sepa… para ustedes.

 

Ya tenemos todo listo. 💬 Ahora es tu turno.

Deja tu pregunta en la sección de comentario👇 o puedes preguntar de forma anónima aquí 👉 AMA Champion Anonymously Questions o escaneando el QR Code 

 

📅Esta edición del AMA estará abierta para preguntas hasta el 27 de marzo de 2026.

Después de esa fecha, las preguntas seleccionadas serán respondidas en un video grabado que se publicará en el canal de YouTube de la SAP Community  👉 @SAPCommunity

También se publicará un blog resumen con las preguntas y respuestas para la comunidad.

 

Cuéntame…

🤓¿Qué tema de SAP te gustaría que exploremos juntos?

entry.title: Clean Core Is a Leadership Discipline, Not a Technical Rule
entry.content:
I recently took an SAP course about Clean Core, and it left me thinking about how people interpret the concept in practice. Most explanations focus on ways to keep the system as simple as possible, avoiding changing the original software too much. Personally, what we keep seeing in real SAP transformations that our clients bring to our table is that Clean Core doesn’t necessarily represent an issue because of architecture, but rather because of people. 

The tension around Clean Core becomes apparent the moment a decision maker asks to adjust something “just a little”, explains that their processes work a bit differently, or insists that there’s only one way things have always been done. Of course, there are several ways to solve the request, but my question is: Should the change be implemented at all in the first place? And here is where our concern starts, because this is not a technical decision, but a leadership one.

Clean Core Is Not About Restricting the Business

Clean Core is sometimes interpreted as saying “no” to the business, as if it were about restricting flexibility or forcing standardization just because. In reality, it has more to do with protecting the future of the system. With the migration to cloud ERP and faster upgrade cycles, customization starts to play a different role. Things that used to be manageable in traditional on-premise systems can become way more complicated in a cloud environment. Besides the problems of costs or upgrades, customizations introduce a deeper issue: it becomes less clear who is responsible for what.

This became clear after assessing many of our clients’ landscapes, where heavily customized processes often resulted in unclear ownership. Who owns the process: the business or IT? Who decides when and what needs to change? Over time, these customizations tend to end up in a strange situation where no one really owns, questions or removes them. 

Clean Core introduces a different kind of conversation before all those issues come up. It pushes organizations to ask whether a customization is necessary at all: Does it really create value or is it just the protection of a legacy habit? Once again, we are talking about leadership decisions that have almost nothing to do with architecture. 

What Pressure to Customize Often Reveals

Across multiple client transformations, what the strongest pressure to customize reveals are organizational dynamics. Sometimes, there are governance gaps, where decisions are made without a clear framework or roadmap. Other times, as mentioned earlier, ownership is unclear and different teams defend their own variations of a process. In other cases, deadlines and internal pressure push teams towards quick fixes as temporary solutions instead of redesigning processes. Finally, sometimes customization simply reflects resistance to change. 

These dynamics don’t disappear just because we introduce better extensibility tools. They tend to improve only when leadership alignment improves, and Clean Core works when teams are clear about when it actually makes sense to move away from standard. The key is being intentional. In the projects we’ve worked on, the teams that manage this better are the ones that pause and ask themselves simple questions like if a customization will create problems later on or if it would make upgrades harder. 

Our Experience

Recently, we worked on a S/4 project for a company that had started using drones to collect field data for forecasting. The goal was to move beyond manual reports and gain more real-time visibility from physical assets. Of course, Clean Core was part of the strategy from the beginning, as the goal was to keep S/4 standard and integrate new capabilities in a sustainable way.

Once the workshops started, the situation became more complicated. The forecasting team suggested adding custom fields in inventory and maintenance documents so the drone data could be stored directly in the S/4 tables. It wasn’t an unreasonable idea, we understood that they just wanted the data where everything else already lived.

With that idea on the table, the conversation started moving forward. If the drone data was already in S/4, why not use it directly in the planning logic? There was even the suggestion of modifying the material planning process so the drone data could automatically adjust demand forecasts. What they were asking for was technically possible, and thinking back on, it would’ve probably been faster to just build it directly in the core. This was exactly the moment where all I’ve stated about Clean Core became a reality for us, we had no other choice but to ask ourselves if this logic should really live inside the S/4 core.

In the end, we decided to keep the drone data outside of S/4 and bring the results back through standard interfaces. Yes, it took a bit more coordination, but the core system stayed clean. 

What that project made clear to us is how these situations usually start. At the beginning everyone agrees with the idea of keeping the system standard. But once the workshops begin, small requests start appearing. None of them sound dramatic and, again, they are technically possible and even make sense. But that’s usually how heavy customization slowly creeps back into the system.

Clean Core as Cultural Discipline

In the end, Clean Core says a lot about how an organization makes decisions. In our experience, it depends less on tools and more on leadership, ownership, and how teams make decisions during a transformation. Staying “clean” is not about having a perfect system, but about pausing before customizing and asking whether the change is truly necessary. 

When Clean Core breaks down, it is rarely because the technology couldn’t support the extension. More often it happens when ownership is unclear or short-term pressure overrides long-term thinking. In that sense, Clean Core ends up revealing how disciplined an organization really is when making technology decisions.
